title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Theo Marsh, who runs a bicycle repair café.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're talking to Theo Marsh, who runs a bicycle repair café. Theo, your café repairs bikes while people have a drink. How did you first become interested in bicycle repair? || Guest: People often think I learnt everything from a cycling club, but that came much later. When I was sixteen, [20]I helped my younger sister fix her bike before a school trip, and I enjoyed solving the problem[/20]. The bike was old, and I only had a simple tool box, but it moved properly again. That small success made me read more about repairs and ask neighbours if I could practise on their bikes. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you were starting out? || Guest: The first few months were exciting, but not easy. I could repair a chain or put on new brake blocks, and customers were patient with me. What slowed me down was something different. [21]Finding the right spare parts was harder than repairing the bikes[/21]. Some older models needed pieces that shops no longer sold. I spent evenings searching online and learning which parts could safely be used instead. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about it now? || Guest: I like teaching people simple things, such as checking tyre pressure, but the best moment comes later. A customer often arrives thinking their bike is finished forever. Then, after an hour, it is ready again. [22]I enjoy seeing people ride away safely on something they thought was useless[/22]. That feeling is better than selling a new bike, because it saves money and keeps things out of the rubbish. || Interviewer: Have you changed the way you work since then? || Guest: Yes, I made a clear mistake in my second summer. I was too positive and wanted everyone to be happy, so [23]I promised to finish too many bikes in one afternoon[/23]. By six o'clock, I still had four bikes waiting, and two people needed them for work the next morning. I apologised and worked late, but I learnt that good service also means being honest about time. || Interviewer: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work? || Guest: The café is better organised now. Before, people just arrived with any problem, and I sometimes discovered that a repair needed parts I did not have. [24]Now I ask customers to send a photo before they come[/24]. It only takes them a minute, but it helps me prepare. I can order a part, choose the right tools and give a more useful price before they travel across town. || Interviewer: And what are your plans for the future? || Guest: I want the café to become more than a repair shop. Lots of young people ride to school, but they cannot fix a flat tyre or adjust a seat. [25]I want to run short repair classes for teenagers[/25]. They would learn in small groups, using real bikes, and the lessons would be cheap. If they feel confident, they may use their bikes more often instead of asking for lifts. || Interviewer: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar? || Guest: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like bicycle repair café, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal. || Interviewer: Thank you, that was really interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're talking to Theo Marsh, who runs a bicycle repair café. Theo, your café repairs bikes while people have a drink. How did you first become interested in bicycle repair?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People often think I learnt everything from a cycling club, but that came much later. When I was sixteen, I helped my younger sister fix her bike before a school trip, and I enjoyed solving the problem. The bike was old, and I only had a simple tool box, but it moved properly again. That small success made me read more about repairs and ask neighbours if I could practise on their bikes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you were starting out?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The first few months were exciting, but not easy. I could repair a chain or put on new brake blocks, and customers were patient with me. What slowed me down was something different. Finding the right spare parts was harder than repairing the bikes. Some older models needed pieces that shops no longer sold. I spent evenings searching online and learning which parts could safely be used instead.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about it now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I like teaching people simple things, such as checking tyre pressure, but the best moment comes later. A customer often arrives thinking their bike is finished forever. Then, after an hour, it is ready again. I enjoy seeing people ride away safely on something they thought was useless. That feeling is better than selling a new bike, because it saves money and keeps things out of the rubbish.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you changed the way you work since then?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, I made a clear mistake in my second summer. I was too positive and wanted everyone to be happy, so I promised to finish too many bikes in one afternoon. By six o'clock, I still had four bikes waiting, and two people needed them for work the next morning. I apologised and worked late, but I learnt that good service also means being honest about time.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The café is better organised now. Before, people just arrived with any problem, and I sometimes discovered that a repair needed parts I did not have. Now I ask customers to send a photo before they come. It only takes them a minute, but it helps me prepare. I can order a part, choose the right tools and give a more useful price before they travel across town.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: And what are your plans for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I want the café to become more than a repair shop. Lots of young people ride to school, but they cannot fix a flat tyre or adjust a seat. I want to run short repair classes for teenagers. They would learn in small groups, using real bikes, and the lessons would be cheap. If they feel confident, they may use their bikes more often instead of asking for lifts.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like bicycle repair café, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Thank you, that was really interesting.

questions.question: What made Theo interested in bicycle repair?

options.A: fixing a bike for someone in his family

questions.explanation: Theo says he became interested after helping his younger sister fix her bike before a school trip.

questions.question: What was difficult for Theo at the beginning?
questions.explanation: Theo says finding the right spare parts was harder than doing the repairs.

questions.question: What does Theo enjoy most about his work?

options.C: watching customers leave on repaired bikes

questions.explanation: Theo enjoys seeing people ride away safely on bikes they thought were useless.

questions.question: What mistake did Theo make in his second summer?

options.A: He accepted too many repairs in one day.
options.B: He forgot to phone several customers.
options.C: He charged too little for difficult work.

questions.explanation: Theo says he promised to finish too many bikes in one afternoon.

questions.question: How has Theo changed the way he works?

options.A: He opens the café earlier in the day.
options.B: He asks for pictures of bikes first.
options.C: He only repairs modern bicycles now.

questions.explanation: Theo now asks customers to send a photo before they come.

questions.question: What does Theo want to do next?
questions.explanation: Theo wants to run short repair classes for teenagers.
